name: Threat Intelligence and Sources
description: Threat intelligence types, sources, and how to apply them to security operations
key_facts: Threat intelligence types: strategic (high-level trends), tactical (TTPs), operational (specific campaigns), technical (IoCs)
STIX (Structured Threat Information eXpression) is a standardized language for sharing threat data
TAXII (Trusted Automated eXchange of Indicator Information) is the transport protocol for STIX data
OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) includes public threat feeds, social media, and government advisories
MITRE ATT&CK framework catalogs adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures
common_misconceptions: Threat intelligence is only for large organizations (organizations of all sizes can benefit from threat feeds and IoCs)
All threat intelligence is equally reliable (sources vary in quality and must be validated)

name: SIEM and Log Analysis
description: Security Information and Event Management systems, log correlation, and security monitoring
key_facts: SIEM aggregates logs from multiple sources and correlates events to detect security incidents
Log sources include firewalls, IDS/IPS, endpoints, authentication systems, and applications
Correlation rules define patterns that indicate potential security incidents
SOAR (Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response) automates incident response workflows
Use cases define detection scenarios: brute force (multiple failed logins), data exfiltration (unusual outbound traffic)
common_misconceptions: SIEM replaces human analysts (SIEM is a tool that requires skilled analysts to tune, investigate, and respond)
More logs always means better security (too many unfiltered logs create noise and alert fatigue)

name: Network Security Monitoring
description: Network traffic analysis, packet capture, and anomaly detection techniques
key_facts: Packet capture tools: Wireshark for analysis, tcpdump for command-line capture
NetFlow/sFlow provides flow-level traffic summaries without full packet capture
Baseline analysis compares current traffic patterns against known-normal behavior
Indicators: beaconing (regular callbacks to C2), DNS tunneling (data in DNS queries), port scanning
Network TAPs and SPAN/mirror ports provide traffic copies for monitoring without affecting flow
common_misconceptions: Encrypted traffic cannot be analyzed (metadata, flow patterns, and TLS certificate details are still visible)
IDS signatures catch all attacks (signature-based detection misses zero-day and novel attacks)

name: Vulnerability Scanning and Assessment
description: Vulnerability scanner configuration, scan types, and assessment methodologies
key_facts: Credentialed scans authenticate to targets and provide deeper results than uncredentialed scans
CVSS scores rate severity: Critical (9.0-10.0), High (7.0-8.9), Medium (4.0-6.9), Low (0.1-3.9)
False positives are reported vulnerabilities that do not exist; validation confirms true positives
Scan scheduling considers maintenance windows, network impact, and compliance requirements
Common scanners: Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, Rapid7 InsightVM
common_misconceptions: Vulnerability scans are the same as penetration tests (scans identify vulnerabilities; pentests actively exploit them)
A clean scan means the system is secure (scanners only detect known vulnerabilities)

name: Vulnerability Remediation and Prioritization
description: Prioritizing vulnerabilities, remediation strategies, and risk-based approaches
key_facts: Prioritization factors: CVSS score, exploitability, asset criticality, exposure level
Remediation options: patching, compensating controls, configuration changes, network segmentation
Compensating controls mitigate risk when direct remediation is not immediately possible
SLA-based remediation timelines: critical (24-72 hours), high (1-2 weeks), medium (30 days), low (90 days)
Exception management documents accepted risks when vulnerabilities cannot be remediated
common_misconceptions: All vulnerabilities must be patched immediately (risk-based prioritization focuses on the most impactful first)
CVSS score alone determines priority (business context and exploitability are equally important)

name: Web Application Vulnerabilities
description: Common web application vulnerabilities and secure coding practices
key_facts: OWASP Top 10 includes injection, broken authentication, XSS, insecure deserialization, and SSRF
SQL injection inserts malicious SQL through user input to read/modify database data
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) injects scripts into web pages viewed by other users
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tricks authenticated users into performing unintended actions
Input validation and parameterized queries are primary defenses against injection attacks
common_misconceptions: Web application firewalls (WAFs) eliminate the need for secure coding (WAFs are a layer of defense, not a replacement)
HTTPS prevents web application attacks (HTTPS encrypts transport but does not prevent application-layer vulnerabilities)

name: Incident Response Process
description: Incident response lifecycle, containment strategies, and recovery procedures
key_facts: IR phases (NIST): Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment/Eradication/Recovery, Post-Incident Activity
Containment strategies: isolate affected systems, block malicious IPs, disable compromised accounts
Evidence preservation requires maintaining chain of custody and creating forensic images
Root cause analysis identifies the attack vector and prevents recurrence
Lessons learned meetings document what happened, what worked, and what needs improvement
common_misconceptions: Rebuilding a compromised system is always necessary (some incidents can be remediated without full rebuild)
Incident response ends when the threat is removed (post-incident review and improvement are essential)

name: Digital Forensics Fundamentals
description: Digital evidence collection, analysis tools, and forensic investigation procedures
key_facts: Order of volatility: registers, cache, RAM, disk, remote logs, archive media
Write blockers prevent modification of original evidence during acquisition
Forensic imaging creates a bit-for-bit copy; hash values verify image integrity
Memory forensics analyzes RAM contents for running processes, network connections, and malware
Timeline analysis reconstructs the sequence of events using file timestamps and log entries
common_misconceptions: Deleting files permanently removes data (deleted files remain on disk until overwritten and can often be recovered)
Live forensics is always preferable (live analysis may alter volatile data; the approach depends on the situation)

name: Security Reporting and Metrics
description: Communicating security findings, metrics, and recommendations to stakeholders
key_facts: KPIs: Mean Time to Detect (MTTD), Mean Time to Respond (MTTR), number of incidents by severity
Executive reports focus on business impact and risk; technical reports provide detailed findings
Vulnerability reports include severity, affected assets, remediation recommendations, and timelines
Dashboards provide real-time visibility into security posture for different audiences
After-action reports document incident details, timeline, impact, and lessons learned
common_misconceptions: Technical details are appropriate for executive reporting (executives need business impact and risk context)
More metrics always provide better insight (focus on actionable metrics that drive security improvements)

name: Compliance Frameworks and Communication
description: Regulatory requirements, compliance reporting, and stakeholder communication
key_facts: PCI DSS requires quarterly vulnerability scans by an Approved Scanning Vendor (ASV)
HIPAA requires breach notification within 60 days of discovery for healthcare data
GDPR requires data breach notification to authorities within 72 hours
Risk registers document identified risks, their likelihood, impact, and mitigation status
Tabletop exercises simulate incidents to test response plans without operational impact
common_misconceptions: Meeting compliance requirements guarantees security (compliance is a minimum baseline, not comprehensive security)
Compliance frameworks are interchangeable (each framework addresses specific regulatory or industry requirements)
